steps_en: Mince fresh apricot skins (depulped) and macerate for one day with 500 g of alcohol and 2 liters of distilled water.
Treat with 2 drops of essential oil of citron (terpene-free) and transfer everything to a glass matrass.
Distill 1 kg; treat the distillate with a mixture of 5 g of vanilla tincture (1:10) and 1 g of saffron tincture (1:10).
Color with caramel tincture.
If cloudiness occurs, eliminate it by allowing to rest for 10 days and filtering through silica sand.

Color the distillate with 0.1 grams of Safranin T extra and 3 drops of saffron tincture (1:10).
Mince 200 grams of fresh, depulped citron peel.
Macerate for one day with 500 grams of alcohol and 1 liter of water.
Mix with 3 drops of terpene-free citron essential oil and 1 drop of terpene-free bitter orange essential oil.
Transfer everything to a glass matrass and distill 1 kg.
Treat the distillate with a mixture of 3 grams of vanilla tincture (1:10), 16 drops of turmeric tincture (1:10), and 20 drops of caramel tincture.
Eliminate any cloudiness by resting for 10 days or filtering through silica.

steps_en: Heat the sugar and water mixture to boiling.
Strain the liquid.
Filter the liquid.
Alternatively, prepare cold using approximately 5% more sugar (1.800 kg sugar to 1 kg water), without heating.
recipes.notes_en: Hot preparation is preferred over cold. Addition of 100-200 g citric acid per quintal of sugar is recommended to prevent crystallization. This concentrated syrup (57 L water per quintal sugar) weighs 157 kg with sugar content of about 93-94%, and can be stored in barrels then diluted with water as needed. Sugar content limits for liqueur classes: Rosoli/Creams 350-450 g/L, Fine Liqueurs 175-325 g/L, Ordinary Liqueurs 50-115 g/L, Aqua vitae 40-85 g/L.

steps_en: In a clean, well-polished tinned copper kettle, mix the sugar, citric acid, and water.
Heat over direct flame, stirring thoroughly so the sugar dissolves completely before boiling begins.
Skim off the foam forming at the surface until the syrup is perfectly clear.
Continue boiling until the syrup begins to thicken, or until large bubbles rise from within the mass and long threads form when lifting the skimming ladle (about 20 minutes).
recipes.notes_en: This method, especially used in France, produces a concentrated syrup corresponding to an equal weight of sugar.

ingredients.note: reconstituted in water; equivalent to one egg white, range 11-4g depending on source quality

steps_en: Place 50 kg raw sugar in an untinned copper bowl.
Add 20 liters water and albumin solution, stirring with wooden spatula until sugar fully dissolves.
Bring rapidly to a boil, pour in 1 liter albumin solution, repeat operation, remove from heat.
Skim off foam, then return to fire.
When boiling, add 3 liters water in several portions, skimming each time.
When syrup is clear and foam-free, filter through cloth.
Syrup should measure 31° Baumé.
recipes.notes_en: Egg whites can be replaced by dried albumin reconstituted in water at a ratio of about 11-4 g per egg white; approximately one egg white is used per 10 kg sugar.

steps_en: Beat egg whites with water.
Pour into sugar placed in a bowl with the remaining albumin solution, reserving 2/3 of it for clarification.
Heat while stirring constantly until sugar fully dissolves.
Add bone black and charcoal.
When syrup boils, pour in reserved albumin solution, bring to boil again, then remove from heat.
After sufficient rest, skim and filter through flannel.
A second filtration is necessary.
recipes.notes_en: This procedure combines decolorization with clarification of colored sugar.

steps_en: Melt 10 kg of refined sugar in 2.6 liters of pure water and 1.2 liters of albuminous water.
Heat and clarify as described previously.
After straining the syrup through wool, add 1 liter of previously filtered triple-distilled orange flower water.
Mix thoroughly and let stand.
recipes.notes_en: An alternative, more delicate method (2°): cold-dissolve 1900 parts white sugar in 100 parts distilled orange flower water and filter through paper. The finished syrup should read 36° (Baumé) when cold.

steps_en: Treat the saffron residue with another 350 g of boiling water.
Press again and combine the two liquids, adding 1 liter of alcohol at 85°.
Treat the remaining saffron residue with 1 liter of alcohol at 85° to exhaust it completely; this alcoholic tincture serves for coloring absinthe.
Saffron tinctures serve for coloring those liqueurs to which the flavor of saffron cannot cause harm; for more delicate ones, recourse is had to another yellow colorant.

steps_en: Macerate 500 g of safflower with 2 liters of water.

Finally add 1 liter of alcohol at 95° and filter the tincture.
recipes.notes_en: Safflower flower contains a yellow substance easily soluble in water and a brown-red substance soluble in alkaline solutions. This tincture combines both extractions.

steps_en: Recipe I: Vigorously incorporate 15 egg yolks (carefully separated from albumen) into a 1-liter bottle with 300 grams of Benedictine liqueur. Fill the bottle with quality cognac.

recipes.notes_en: The pits impart the pleasant bitterness characteristic of this liqueur, as well as the aroma, which is partly due to a very small amount of hydrocyanic acid (prussic acid). Whether using a naked-flame still or a steam still (the latter being preferable), ensure the apparatus is made of or lined with tin or well-tinned copper. For aging and storage: keep Kirsch in glass vessels, covering the opening with a porous material that permits slight evaporation during the first year—this allows acrid principles to volatilize, leaving a very pleasant liquid. Then seal tightly. If sufficient glass vessels are not available, store in ash wood barrels, which have the advantage of not coloring the liquid. In the region, it is customary to store Kirsch during the first year in cellars where the mild temperature favors proper aging.

Slivovitz is a product of Austria-Hungary, distilled from plums (sometimes with added wine or grape refuse) after fermentation. steps_en: Macerate the botanicals in alcohol and water.

Separately dissolve 4 kg of refined sugar in 2 liters of water and concentrate the syrup to 2 liters (without citric acid).
Mix this syrup in a kettle with 4 liters of alcohol (90°) and pour the mixture while still warm into cylindrical or prismatic bottles of approximately 500 ml capacity, filling to about one-third.
Stopper bottles lightly and let rest for some time.

recipes.title_en: Morello Cherry and Raspberry Liqueur (Method III)

ingredients.name_en: Leaves of the same plant (cherry/raspberry leaves)

ingredients.note: added at the end once the liqueur has cooled

steps_en: Ferment 180 parts of sour/morello cherries with 10-30 parts of raspberries and 10-12 parts of leaves of the same plant.
When fermentation has reached the proper stage, distill the liquid together with a little peach kernel and 1 part of crushed Florentine iris root.
Alternatively, macerate the crushed fruit and other ingredients for two or three days with 160 to 200 parts of wine spirit, then distill in a bain-marie still to recover all the spirit used.
If the liqueur is not strong enough, rectify it again on fresh material; this yields a liqueur with a more delicate and more fragrant aroma.
When rectifying, a little walnut and a little iris root may be added to the still.
After a few hours, once the liqueur has cooled, add the syrup.

steps_en: Prepare the apricot extract by selecting 7 kg of ripe, healthy apricots. Clean, peel, and remove the pits.
Crush the fruit and boil with 4 liters of sugar syrup (4 kg sugar).
After cooling, decant the apricot syrup and pour 7 liters of 60% fine alcohol over the fruit residue, stirring well.
Macerate for 5–6 days, then combine with the syrup obtained earlier and filter.
Crush the reserved pits and mix with 1.5 kg of dried Turkish prunes. Macerate in 12 liters of 60% fine alcohol for 12–15 days.
Decant the liquid and combine with the syrup from the first extraction. Filter again and reserve.
Mix the apricot extract (5.5 L), 90° alcohol (5.5 L), strawberry juice (0.5 L), white wine (1 L), and simple syrup (7.5 L).
The final product yields 20 liters at 30% alcohol with a light red color.
recipes.notes_en: The apricot extract is prepared separately over two stages: a primary syrup extraction by boiling, followed by alcohol maceration of the fruit solids and pit infusion.

steps_en: Cook a mixture of 10 kg sugar and 5 liters of cherry juice, reducing to 10 liters.

steps_en: Cook a mixture of 10 kg sugar, 5 liters of raspberry juice, and 10 grams of citric acid, reducing to 10 liters.

Mix thoroughly to incorporate all ingredients.
recipes.notes_en: The citric acid enhances the natural acidity of the raspberry juice. Acetic ether contributes flavor complexity and helps preserve the liqueur.

This method is therefore not well-suited to bitter preparation.

recipes.notes_en: Recipe refers back to the Rum Punch method; no separate ingredient list given.

steps_en: See Rum Punch.

recipes.title_it: (nota a piè di pagina, senza titolo)

yield.note: from 30 liters of pure raspberry juice; alternative method yields 15 liters

steps_en: Distill carefully and cautiously in the still 30 liters of pure raspberry juice, to collect 20 liters of product.
Alternatively, mix in the still's boiler 2 kg of freshly pressed raspberry pomace with 500 g of magnesia and 5 liters of 85° alcohol, and distill slowly to collect 15 liters of product.
recipes.notes_en: This is a footnote continuing from a recipe on the previous page (likely a raspberry liqueur/spirit); the main recipe text is not shown on this page.

steps_en: Place iron rods over a large, wide earthenware vessel.
Place 1 kg of sugar on the rods and drench it with a bottle of good-quality rum.
Ignite the rum and allow it to burn completely until the sugar drips into the vessel.
Pour over two bottles of good table wine and one liter of water or tea along with the juice of one lemon and one orange.
Expose the vessel in a warm (not very hot) oven for at least two hours.
Stir well and serve hot.
recipes.notes_en: A theatrical flaming punch preparation. The sugar caramelizes as the rum burns.

steps_en: Beat 250 g of sugar with 10–12 egg yolks.
Add rum or arrack to taste (at most 4 tablespoons).
Add 0.75 liters of unsweetened cream.
Beat everything to stiff peaks for 18 minutes.
Place the bowl in an ice bath for several hours before serving.
recipes.notes_en: A creamy, frozen punch similar to eggnog.

steps_en: Beat 12 egg yolks with 500 g of fine powdered sugar, a pinch of cinnamon, and half a ground nutmeg.
Slowly pour in a bottle of good rum or arrack while continuously stirring.
While stirring, add one liter of cream.
Beat for 10 minutes.
Pass through a fine sieve.
Serve cold.
recipes.notes_en: A spiced cream punch with rum or arrack.

steps_en: Infuse 10 g of Pekoe tea in 0.5 liter of boiling water and strain.
Squeeze the zest of one lemon over 750 g of sugar, crush it, and beat it with 0.25 liter of water.
Add half a vanilla pod, 8 g of dried orange blossoms, and a pinch of salt to the sugar mixture.
Cover the bowl and heat gently for about 30 minutes.
Strain through a fine sieve.
Combine the liquid with the tea.
Add one bottle of Bordeaux wine, half a bottle of Madeira, half a bottle of arrack, and the juice of four oranges.
recipes.notes_en: A traditional English engagement party punch. Named for the occasion ('dei fidanzati' = 'of the betrothed').

steps_en: Infuse the tea in 800 cc of boiling water; let cool and strain, pressing the tea.
Pour the brandy, rum, and concentrated lemon alcoholate into a covered vessel.
Add the tea infusion, clarified sugar, and citric acid previously dissolved in a little water.
Mix everything thoroughly and color with caramel tincture as needed.
Clarify with gelatin and filter.
recipes.notes_en: Two proportions given (Fine and Ordinario columns). The second method uses fresh lemon zest (1 kg, white part removed, cut into pieces and soaked in boiling water for 24 hours before adding to rum, cognac, fresh lemon juice, and syrup); color should be light brown.
Two proportions given (Ordinario and Fine columns). Ingredients to be combined and likely macerated or infused to produce a concentrated tea extract.

steps_en: Dissolve the sugar in approximately 1.5 litres of water, heating to boiling to obtain 4 litres of syrup.
Add 6–8 grams of pure citric acid to prevent separation of crystals.
Allow the mass to cool almost completely.
Gradually add the arack and alcohol, stirring constantly to combine.
recipes.notes_en: For a finer extract, use the best quality arack, cognac, or rum and superior-quality ingredients. The genuine arack may be replaced with an aromatic imitation product composed of: 940 g arack extract, 10 g vanilla extract, and 50 g genuine arack.

steps_en: Macerate the aromatic mixture in white wine for 5 to 10 days.
Decant and clarify using fish glue (isinglass).
Allow to rest for 15 days.
Add 2 liters of bitter almond kernel tincture (toasted) or 3 liters of good cognac to obtain a first-quality Vermouth.
recipes.notes_en: This recipe is presented as a guideline. Each manufacturer should modify it according to customer taste, production requirements, and especially the quality of the primary materials used (wine must be appropriate for the purpose, genuine, and matching the required alcohol content and sugar content). The resulting product may not be identical to well-known commercial brands.

steps_en: Macerate all ingredients for 5 days.
Decant and clarify with isinglass (fish glue).
After a further 8 days of rest, decant again.
Bottle.
recipes.notes_en: If the product is too bitter, correct it with: 50 L bitter vermouth, 42 L common white wine, 4 L grape sugar, 1 L tincture of bitter almond peel, 4 L 85° alcohol, and golden yellow (caramel) for color. Clarify twice.

steps_en: Mix 19 L Asti wine with 1 L 60° alcohol.
Add all botanical ingredients.
Macerate for 1 month.
Combine all herbs and botanicals.
Add Cognac at 50° and white wine.
Macerate for two months, stirring the mass every 2–3 days.
Decant and clarify.
recipes.notes_en: The recipe specifies maceration in a mixture of Cognac and white wine with dissolved sugar. Stir regularly during the two-month maceration period.

steps_en: Boil the herbs in mature white wine must (intended for sweet wine, but not muscatel) during fermentation.
recipes.notes_en: These herbs are added to white wine must while it is fermenting. The wine should be mature white grape must suitable for sweet wine production (but not muscatel type).

steps_en: Place these sachets in maceration within the base until the aroma of the herbs is no longer perceptible.
Press well.
Filter everything.
recipes.notes_en: The sachet is one of four numbered preparations used in maceration within the vermouth base.

steps_en: Place these sachets in maceration within the base until the aroma of the herbs is no longer perceptible.

steps_en: Place the crushed herbs and spices in a heated copper vessel equipped with a lid and stopcock at the bottom.
Pour boiling water over the herbs.
Close the lid tightly.
After 24 hours, add the spirit.
Close and let rest for 8 days.
recipes.notes_en: The extract is prepared in a copper displacement vessel (with stopcock). Crushed herbs and spices are infused with boiling water, then spirit is added after 24 hours.

steps_en: After 8 days, open the tap and draw off the liquid.
Press the herbs thoroughly.
Combine the pressed herbs with the drawn liquid.
Filter through paper and bottle.
The older this extract becomes, the better the Vermouth will be.
recipes.notes_en: This is a macerated herbal extract base for Vermouth production. Aging improves quality.
ocr_flags: Clima (possibly 'Clima' or corruption of another citrus name)

steps_en: Macerate for one week.
Continue with successive digestion.
Strain the liquid and press the residue.
Combine the strained and pressed liquids and filter completely.
Pour this extract into a hectoliter of good wine.
Let rest for several months.
recipes.notes_en: This extract is combined with wine and aged to develop Vermouth character.

steps_en: Immerse the stated herbs in 10 liters of 85° alcohol.
Macerate for 8 days.
Add the remaining herbs.
Dilute to 35 liters using 18 liters of [water, as continued on next page].
recipes.notes_en: Complex herbal formula with balanced proportions of roots, seeds, and aromatic herbs. Recipe continues on following page.

recipes.title_en: Vermouth Extract – Continued Instructions (from previous page)

steps_en: Put 95° alcohol and 7 liters of common wine into the still; if the still is not available, before adding the 18 liters of alcohol, pour the boiling wine over the mixed herbs and spices instead.
When cooled, add the remaining 18 liters (of alcohol).
Let rest for a further 15 days, stirring every day.
Then press under the press.
To prepare Vermouth, use this extract at a rate of 1½ to 2 liters per hectoliter.

steps_en: Macerate the ground herbs and spices in 1 liter of cognac for 8 days.
Press, and to the pressed liquid add the essential oils, coumarin, sweet spirit of nitre, and acetic ether.
Add the white wine and sugar.
After a few days' rest in a cool place, filter.
recipes.notes_en: Recipe attributed to Dieterich; second stage additions are dissolved/mixed into the pressed macerate before dilution with wine and sugar.

steps_en: Combine the listed herbs in the given proportions per each of the five variant formulas (VI, VII, VIII, IX, X).

steps_en: Pour the wine into a fairly large vessel and add the extract little by little, stirring constantly.
Taste periodically; when the wine has the desired aroma, stop adding extract.
Sweeten (syrup) to taste, and the Vermouth is finished.
Store, even for years, in casks or demijohns, without neglecting to top up the containers.
With aging, the Vermouth refines/improves.
recipes.notes_en: Table presents five proportional variants of a compound aromatic extract used as a base for Vermouth; alcohol at 60° is used as the extraction solvent. Source page 675; recipes attributed to Arnaldo Strucchi's monograph on Vermouth di Torino, Casale Monferrato, 1906.

steps_en: Macerate 3 kg Calissaia cinchona bark in 25 litres of 95° alcohol at cool temperature for 15 days.
After 15 days, draw off 2 to 2½ litres of the resulting tincture.
Dissolve 5 kg powdered sugar in 5 litres of water to form a syrup (yields 8 litres).
Combine the cinchona tincture with the sugar syrup, 1.5 litres vermouth extract, and 89 litres common vermouth.
Color the mixture with cochineal red and caramel to desired shade.
The final product should total 100 litres.
recipes.notes_en: This type of vermouth is more aromatic, bitter, and tonic than common vermouth. Some producers prepare a special extract by adding Calissaia cinchona extract to the maceration of herbs and drugs.

steps_en: Combine 90 litres common vermouth wine with 8 litres old Barolo wine.
Add 4 kg powdered sugar, 2 litres vermouth extract, and 1 litre alcohol.
Mix thoroughly to obtain 100 litres total product.
recipes.notes_en: Although the sweet, dessert-wine character of Barolo may seem incompatible with the bitter, aromatic profile of an apéritif vermouth, this specialized blend has gained considerable commercial importance, particularly for export. Alternatively, prepare using any of the previously indicated vermouth recipes, substituting genuine red wine for white wine, or a mixture of equal parts of both.

steps_en: Take 5–10 litres of your preferred Bitters (Amaro).
Dissolve 5 kg sugar in the Vermouth.
Add to one hectolitre (100 litres) total volume.
Colour with cochineal red and caramel.
recipes.notes_en: Called 'American' because in the United States it is customary to drink Vermouth mixed with bitters and gin (Whiskey), forming a drink called 'Cocktail'. Many different preparations are possible depending on the type of Bitters employed, as there are countless varieties with different flavours, though all based on a bitter foundation.

steps_en: Macerate mace, Ceylon cinnamon, Tonka beans, and saffron threads in cold 95° alcohol.
After 15 days, decant the liquid.
Dissolve white sugar in water.
Add the dissolved sugar and water to the alcoholic tincture.
Mix with 90 litres of common Vermouth.
Filter to obtain 100 litres total of finished Vermouth with Garus.
recipes.notes_en: Garus is an elixir that produces a Vermouth with a very delicate, aromatic flavour.

steps_en: Dissolve the nux vomica tincture (or alcoholic extract) in 100 g of Marsala wine.
Add this solution to 1 litre of plain vermouth.
Add 100 g of sugar.
recipes.notes_en: This preparation is medicinal in nature and must be used with caution.

After several days, filter to obtain 100 litres of finished product.
recipes.notes_en: This produces an excellent vanilla vermouth with delicate flavour and aroma, particularly suited to ladies' consumption.

Add 1.5 litres of alcohol.
Add 1.5 litres of vanilla spirit (prepared by dissolving 100 grams fine vanilla, minced, in 1 litre of absolute alcohol).
recipes.notes_en: Simpler method for producing vanilla Vermouth.

steps_en: Take any good quality Vermouth and bottle it in sturdy bottles.

After 48 hours of maceration, during which the liquid should be shaken frequently, add the wine.
Shake vigorously.
After 8 days the wine is ready to drink.
Do not filter it, as leaving it in contact with the bark allows better extraction of its active principles.
Each time you use it, decant carefully the required quantity.
recipes.notes_en: This is a very tonic wine. Filtering is not recommended, as prolonged contact with the bark ensures better extraction of active compounds.
